I'll try and explain this the best I can.

I think @tobes wanted us to go in a direction where we don't script anything in the code by exposing everything (if possible) as placeholders then users can manipulate the `if`, `else`, etc in the `format` instead of making pull requests. See issue... https://github.com/ultrabug/py3status/issues/752

If you want to, try `window_manipulation`.... That module contains an amount of placeholders and I was able to manipulate lot of things.... With more placeholders, we can do more neat things... without touching the code.

The key is essentially to get all placeholders we can get our hands on in all modules... and have @tobes become a farmer where he can grow the formatters this summer.

Users want colors. Users don't want colors. We can make both things possible.
Users want urgent. Users don't want urgent. We can make both things possible.
Users want floats or ints... We can make both things possible.
Users want to hide or scrub things. We're going to try and make that possible.
Users... et cetera.

This requires lot of changes though... For instance, all modules should stop returning `"color": color` because `module { color = '#ffccff'}` wouldn't work with that since it's hardcoded... Like I said, lot of changes.
* Remove hardcoded color returns.
* Merge separate formats into one `format`. 
* Undo any `int(floating_value)`.
* How to deprecate things properly? I hate this.
* We can compare basic words. `\?if=class=VIP Good Boy!`
* Remove configs like `hide_if_zero` in favor of `'\?if=apt {apt} update}'` or `'\?not_zero {apt} update'`. 

Users does not know about those things too... due to lack of docs and whatnot. Users may not understand that amount of customization too... so I have been trying to come up with decent examples for users to copy and paste into their formats... and make small changes from there.

One problem I think is that @ultrabug may be wanting to keep things stable and make it easy for users to change settings via configs instead of scriptable `format`.

Another problem, I make many PRs. It scares and/or annoys them.
Another problem, they're slow on reviewing and/or merging. It annoys me.

Disclaimer: There are lot of things I don't know about py3status or python... and most of time, I'm just guessing things... and how things work around here... and I'm always doing something wrong too. I'm just a contributor here. Same as you.

yeah it's all judgement calls, but the lighter each update is the better

We should always use the kwarg names when passing kwargs to a function. Using kwargs implies that we may reorder the kwargs later. I.e., use `check_ignore(code, ignore_names=['funny'], ignore_decorators=['f?o*', 'ba[rz]'])`. I'd just use `def check_ignore(code, ignore_names, ignore_decorators, expected):` to make things easier and explicit.

As to why I'm using `reversed`: we only care about the entries at the end (the latest entries), so we want to start with those first. So rather than processing over 1000 entries, hopefully we just process 1.

As to why not enumerate: `reversed` takes a sequence and returns an iterator and `enumerate` actually returns an enumerator (not a sequence). So you'd have to convert the enumerator back to a list. Something like:
    `for build_index, build in reversed(list(enumerate(parser.entries))):` 
Which seems both unintuitive and wasteful for a list of > 1000 entries. Of course I could be convinced otherwise!

That feels wrong to me, but I guess it's ok in python, since they have the unlimited size long type.  20! is the largest value that can be stored in an int (C long).  Above that, python switches to its extended precision long.
```
>>> math.factorial(20)
2432902008176640000
>>> math.factorial(21)
51090942171709440000L
>>> type(math.factorial(21))
<type 'long'>
>>> type(math.factorial(20))
<type 'int'>
```
So in C, n>20 would fail with the naive code.  But I would suspect that this is also the transition to where in Python, the naive code gets slower.  Maybe preserve the old code in a branch `if n > 20`?  Or do you think this is not particularly helpful.  (Premature optimization and all that.)

Mypy bug: https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/3061

```
$ cat /tmp/a.py
from typing import Any


class A:
    def __init__(self, x):
        # type: (int) -> None
        self.x = x

    def __eq__(self, other):
        # type: (Any) -> bool
        if not isinstance(other, self.__class__):
            return False
        return self.x == other.x

$ mypy /tmp/a.py
/tmp/a.py:13: error: "object" has no attribute "x"
```

vs.

```
$ cat /tmp/a.py
from typing import Any


class A:
    def __init__(self, x):
        # type: (int) -> None
        self.x = x

    def __eq__(self, other):
        # type: (Any) -> bool
        if not isinstance(other, A):
            return False
        return self.x == other.x
$ mypy /tmp/a.py
$
```

Third party library support should have non-None default value as the library has the context of what is captured. For example, `boto` defaults the namespace to `aws` and `requests` defaults the namespace to `remote`. The aiohttp client is used for making outbound calls so it should be default to `remote` with the option to be set to something else (although I cannot think of any use case that anyone needs to do that).

Setting namespace to `remote` makes sure the service graph renders a node for that endpoint. The X-Ray backend only renders a node when 1. that node actively sends segment or 2. the namespace of the subsegment representing that node is `aws` or `remote`. To be more specific, if you use the client to call a service `B` which you don't own, then `B` is unlikely to generate any trace data. If this call is marked as `remote`, you can see `B` on your service graph.

The only case where you might want to mark an out-going http call subsegment to `local` depends on the http client implementation. If the client thrown a `ClientError` when the call cannot even go out due to connection timeout, DNS lookup fails, invalid input parameters etc, you might want to flip it back to `local` as the error has nothing to do with the downstream service. This is most time a bonus as a lot of libraries doesn't let you easily categorize if the error is from local or from downstream.

* These "registrations" are in alphabetical order, to ease finding them later, which is a common occurrence. The "first key" is the module (`from .bookmark ...`) so these belong up with the 'b's.
* I assume you got the spelling remedied later, this is an example of where I can't easily tell because it may be in a later commit.
* The imports are also made to appear in alphabetical order (case and leading underscore insensitive). So they should appear as CT_BookmarkEnd, CT_BookmarkStart (reverse of current order).
* The `register_element_cls()` calls also appear in alphabetical order (by tag name), which eases detection of duplicates.
* This might give you an idea of the level to which conventions have established in this project. There are a lot of them you'll learn along the way. Together they make for rapid navigation and detection of errors. I know they can seem excessive to some folks, depending on one's disposition for extreme order :)

I talked with Mark about this, and I think `__call__` is more flexible here - it can take parameters, and can be changed and added to later, while `__iter__` locks you into a particular way of doing things that can't be changed easily.  Also, doing it this way lets us only construct the iterator once, with whatever state is necessary (e.g., batch size, dynamic padding, adaptive batch size...), and use it for all datasets (training, validation, whatever).  The reason we had the ugly `last_num_batches` thing was to ensure that we could keep track of an epoch, and only iterate over a dataset exactly once.  I just added a `yield_one_pass` function to do that instead, as well as a `num_batches_per_epoch` function, if you don't want to use `yield_one_pass`.

All of this logic makes sense, but it's frustrating, since random_split works by generating a random Boolean value for each row and then performing a filter internally, before returning the two resulting SArrays. We're having to re-implement so much of this operation, and the is_in case requires a linear search through `not_chosen` for each row, and the filter case requires sorting.

Could we somehow generate a random boolean for each row that yields the same result for each session ID? In principle, all it would take is a suitable hash function:

```
chosen_filter = data[session_id].apply(lambda x: hash_fn(x + seed) < fraction * MAX_HASH_FN_OUTPUT)
train = data[chosen_filter]
valid = data[1 - chosen_filter]
```

The total operation should then be linear, at face value. All that is required is a suitable hash function. For `hash_fn(x)` it may even suffice to re-initialize a RNG with `x` as the seed, perform one draw from uniform(0,1) and compare against `fraction`.

If there's something I'm missing, or if Python doesn't really make it easy enough to do the above, I could always investigate this approach as a followup someday. How long does this approach take in practice?

    try:
        from pywt import dwt, idwt

> Why is the condition necessary here? 

This is guarding the event `SendRevealSecret`, so that we only register the secret and send the secret reveal message once.

> And from my understanding waiting_for_secret would be a better name for the variable!?

This PR fixes the bug where a node spams another with secret reveal messages, because of that the variable was named `should_send_secret`, because we only want to do it once. It just happens that for the existing states we only send the reveal when the target is at `secret_request` state.

I am not sure what you are saying here. `basicConfig` is to configure the root logger to some sane defaults. (The root logger is the one at the top of hierarchy). Like attaching a stream handler and setting some format string and level.

If you want to configure individual logger directly, then you can fetch its instance and configure it on that. Or you use `dictConfig` for a more descriptive way to do it. There are different way to configure stuff. You can even use exiting support for config files. Or even do it remotely over the network. It is pretty crazy what is possible.
